name: Massachusetts
tooltip: Massachusetts — Cat 2: Privacy-tort statute, no post-mortem term. 2024 deepfake NCII statute extends to AI.
rightOfPublicity: Limited. Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 214, § 3A is a codified commercial-appropriation statute covering name, portrait, and picture. **Important caveats: no voice coverage, no signature coverage, no post-mortem term, structurally a privacy tort rather than a true property right.**
postMortem: No codified post-mortem term. § 3A is silent on descendibility.
aiCoverage: Yes. Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 272 §§ 105, 105D as amended by St. 2024 c. 117 (effective Sept. 18, 2024) expressly extends to "digitization" — defined to include computer-generated and AI-altered images. Suno is currently a major AI music litigation venue based in the District of Massachusetts.
remedy: Civil suit under § 3A for actual damages and injunctive relief. For AI-generated NCII, criminal referral plus civil action under ch. 272 § 105D.
whatsInteresting: Massachusetts is the closest call in this whole categorization. § 3A is structurally a New York-style privacy statute, not a true right of publicity — no voice, no post-mortem, no descendibility. But the 2024 amendments to the NCII statute expressly include AI-generated/altered images, which is a real AI overlay on a real codified base. If you're a performer based in Boston, your statutory likeness protection is thinner than in New York or California — but better than common-law-only states.
